title: Add 1Password and 1password-cli to Brewfile
description: 1Password is a password manager with a CLI (op) that integrates with direnv for secrets injection and SSH agent for key management. Add 'cask 1password' and 'brew 1password-cli' to .Brewfile. The CLI enables 'op run --' prefix for secret injection in dev workflows.

title: Add macOS system defaults script
description: Create scripts/macos-defaults.sh with idempotent defaults write commands for key repeat speed, Finder settings (show hidden files, path bar, sort folders first), Dock (autohide, tile size), screenshot location (~/Screenshots), and disabling .DS_Store on network volumes. Document that it requires re-login or killall for some settings to take effect. Wire into install.sh as an optional step or run manually.

title: Add ~/.ssh/config template to dotfiles
description: An SSH config template is not tracked. Add dotfiles/.ssh/config with common settings: ServerAliveInterval, AddKeysToAgent, UseKeychain (macOS), IdentityFile defaults, and commented placeholder host entries. Symlink via install.sh with appropriate 600 permissions handling.

title: Add karabiner-elements config to dotfiles
description: karabiner-elements is installed and in use but its config (~/.config/karabiner/karabiner.json) is not tracked. Add to dotfiles as dotfiles/.config/karabiner/karabiner.json and symlink via install.sh. Preserves key remapping rules across reinstalls.

title: Add .gitignore_global to dotfiles
description: A global gitignore is not currently tracked. Add dotfiles/.gitignore_global covering: .DS_Store, .envrc, *.pyc, __pycache__, .venv, node_modules, .mise.toml local overrides, editor swap files (.swp, .swo), and OS/IDE artifacts. Set core.excludesfile in .gitconfig to point to it.

title: Add .gitconfig to dotfiles
description: A global .gitconfig is not currently tracked in the bootstrap repo. Add dotfiles/.gitconfig with: user identity, default branch (main), push behavior (simple or current), rebase-on-pull, delta as core.pager, common aliases (lg, st, co, etc.), and merge/diff tool settings. Symlink via install.sh.

title: Add mise to Brewfile and evaluate replacing pyenv/nvm/rbenv
description: mise is a unified runtime version manager that replaces pyenv, nvm, rbenv, and others with a single tool and .mise.toml config files. It is already referenced in .gitignore, suggesting partial adoption. Add to .Brewfile, add shell activation to .zsh/mise.zsh, and evaluate whether to deprecate individual version manager files (pyenv.zsh, nvm.zsh, rbenv.zsh). Keep existing managers until fully migrated.

title: Add direnv to Brewfile and configure shell hook
description: direnv auto-loads and unloads environment variables from .envrc files when entering/leaving directories. Essential for managing per-project secrets and env config without polluting the global shell. Add to .Brewfile and add eval hook in .zsh/zzzz_after.zsh.

title: Update CLAUDE.md and README.md for new repo structure
description:
Rewrite CLAUDE.md and README.md to reflect the symlink-based structure, removing Ansible references and documenting the new workflow.

Steps:
- Step 1: Update CLAUDE.md 'What This Is' section — remove Ansible description, describe symlink approach
- Step 2: Update CLAUDE.md 'Commands' section — replace ansible commands with make install, make diff, make brew-sync, make brew-dump
- Step 3: Update CLAUDE.md 'Architecture' section — document dotfiles/, hosts/, install.sh, Makefile structure
- Step 4: Remove or replace Ansible-specific tags and uv/mise setup instructions in CLAUDE.md
- Step 5: Update (or create) README.md with: repo purpose, prerequisites, make install usage, Brewfile drift workflow, per-machine override instructions

Acceptance criteria:
- AC1: CLAUDE.md contains no references to ansible/, uv, mise, or Ansible commands
- AC2: CLAUDE.md 'Commands' section accurately describes make targets and quick zsh copy
- AC3: README.md explains the symlink approach and how to set up a new machine
- AC4: Architecture section documents dotfiles/, hosts/<machine>/, install.sh, and Makefile

title: Remove vestigial directories: ansible/, system/, apps.md
description:
Delete the Ansible-based infrastructure and stale files that the symlink approach replaces.

Steps:
- Step 1: Delete ansible/ directory entirely
- Step 2: Delete system/ directory entirely
- Step 3: Delete apps.md (references stale tools like Atom and YADR)
- Step 4: Update .gitignore if it references removed paths
- Step 5: Verify keyboardio/ is untouched

Acceptance criteria:
- AC1: ansible/ directory no longer exists in the repo
- AC2: system/ directory no longer exists in the repo
- AC3: apps.md no longer exists in the repo
- AC4: keyboardio/ is unchanged
- AC5: No dangling references to removed paths in remaining files

title: Create Makefile with install and brew targets
description:
Write a Makefile at repo root with five targets covering install, diff, and Brewfile drift management.

Steps:
- Step 1: Add 'make install' target → runs ./install.sh
- Step 2: Add 'make diff' target → runs git diff (live files are repo files)
- Step 3: Add 'make brew-install' target → runs brew bundle install --global
- Step 4: Add 'make brew-sync' target → dumps to /tmp/.Brewfile.current, diffs vs dotfiles/.Brewfile (read-only drift view)
- Step 5: Add 'make brew-dump' target → runs brew bundle dump --force (writes live state to ~/.Brewfile → repo file)

Acceptance criteria:
- AC1: make install invokes ./install.sh
- AC2: make diff runs git diff
- AC3: make brew-install runs brew bundle install --global
- AC4: make brew-sync shows bidirectional drift between live brew state and dotfiles/.Brewfile without modifying anything
- AC5: make brew-dump writes live brew state to ~/.Brewfile (which is the symlinked repo file)

title: Create install.sh symlink installer
description:
Write install.sh at repo root. Detects machine via hostname -s, symlinks individual files (not directories), backs up conflicts, applies shared dotfiles then host-specific overrides.

Steps:
- Step 1: Detect machine name via hostname -s; check if hosts/<machine>/ exists
- Step 2: For each file under dotfiles/, compute target path (strip dotfiles/ prefix, prepend ~/); apply symlink logic
- Step 3: Symlink logic per file: (a) target absent → create parent dirs + symlink; (b) already symlink into this repo → skip; (c) existing file/foreign symlink → rename to <target>.bak.<timestamp> then symlink
- Step 4: After shared files, process hosts/<machine>/ with same logic, overriding shared files of same name
- Step 5: Script must be idempotent (safe to run multiple times)

Acceptance criteria:
- AC1: Running install.sh on mbp2022 creates symlinks for all files under dotfiles/ into ~/
- AC2: Running install.sh twice produces no errors and skips already-correct symlinks
- AC3: A pre-existing ~/file gets renamed to ~/file.bak.<timestamp> before the symlink is created
- AC4: Script does NOT install software, run brew bundle, or touch anything outside ~/
- AC5: If hosts/<machine>/ does not exist, only shared dotfiles are installed (no error)

title: Migrate dotfiles from ansible/ to dotfiles/ directory
description:
Move all shared config files from ansible/roles/workstations/files/ into a new dotfiles/ directory mirroring ~/. Also create hosts/mbp2022/ placeholder and dotfiles/.config/ghostty/ placeholder.

Steps:
- Step 1: Create dotfiles/ directory structure (dotfiles/.zshrc, dotfiles/.zsh/, dotfiles/.zsh.before/, dotfiles/.zsh.after/, dotfiles/.vimrc, dotfiles/.Brewfile, dotfiles/.config/ghostty/)
- Step 2: Move zsh/.zshrc → dotfiles/.zshrc
- Step 3: Move zsh/.zsh/*.zsh → dotfiles/.zsh/*.zsh
- Step 4: Move zsh/.zsh.before/ → dotfiles/.zsh.before/
- Step 5: Move zsh/.zsh.after/ → dotfiles/.zsh.after/
- Step 6: Move vim/.vimrc → dotfiles/.vimrc
- Step 7: Move homebrew/.Brewfile-mac → dotfiles/.Brewfile
- Step 8: Create hosts/mbp2022/ empty placeholder directory
- Step 9: Create dotfiles/.config/ghostty/ placeholder
- Step 10: Verify dotfiles/.zsh/secrets.zsh sources from ~/.secrets (never from the repo) — credentials must never be committed

Acceptance criteria:
- AC1: All files from ansible/roles/workstations/files/ exist at their new paths under dotfiles/
- AC2: hosts/mbp2022/ directory exists (can be empty)
- AC3: dotfiles/.config/ghostty/ placeholder exists
- AC4: No files in the old ansible/ paths are missing from dotfiles/
- AC5: secrets.zsh (if present) sources credentials from ~/.secrets, not from any path inside the repo

title: Import ai-config repo contents into bootstrap repo
description:
Move AI tooling config files from ~/code/ai-configs/ into this bootstrap repo so they're managed here and symlinked into place by install.sh.

Source repo: ~/code/ai-configs/ (tools.json defines current install targets)

Files to move into dotfiles/:
- claude/CLAUDE.md          → dotfiles/.claude/CLAUDE.md          (symlinks to ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md)
- claude/settings.json      → dotfiles/.claude/settings.json      (symlinks to ~/.claude/settings.json)
- claude/statusline-command.sh → dotfiles/.claude/statusline-command.sh (symlinks to ~/.claude/statusline-command.sh)
- claude/rules/             → dotfiles/.claude/rules/              (files symlink to ~/.claude/rules/*)
- opencode/opencode.jsonc   → dotfiles/.config/opencode/opencode.jsonc (symlinks to ~/.config/opencode/opencode.jsonc)

install.sh already handles symlinking all files under dotfiles/ to ~/. No changes to install.sh needed — just placing files in the right dotfiles/ subdirectories is sufficient.

After moving files, verify symlinks exist at their target locations and update ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md if it currently points at the ai-configs repo version.

title: Import Terminal configuration into bootstrap
description: Add terminal emulator configuration files to the bootstrap repo so terminal settings are managed as dotfiles and deployed via install.sh. Determine which terminal(s) are in use (Terminal.app, iTerm2, Ghostty, Warp, etc.) and locate their config files for inclusion under dotfiles/ or hosts/mbp2022/.

title: Check and update system hostnames
description: Audit current hostnames for all managed systems (mbp2022, lemp9) and ensure they are correctly configured. Verify that hostname -s returns the expected values since install.sh uses this for per-machine override detection.

title: Dotfiles redesign: symlink-based config management
description: Eliminate config drift by making repo files the live files via symlinks. Editing ~/.zshrc becomes editing the repo file — no sync step needed. Replaces the Ansible-based approach with a lightweight install.sh + Makefile workflow. Covers zsh, vim, Homebrew Brewfile, and app configs (ghostty). Per-machine overrides supported under hosts/<machine>/.

title: Rename master to main and update GitHub
description: Rename the default branch from 'master' to 'main' in both the local repo and on GitHub. Update any references to 'master' in config files, CI, or documentation.
close_reason: Local branch renamed master->main, upstream tracking updated to origin/main, CLAUDE.md updated

title: Evolve tmux-start: add --no-restore and --override flags
description:
## Why

tmux-start currently always runs tmux-resurrect restore and always skips bootstrapping if a 'bootstrap' session already exists. There are two missing escape hatches:

1. **No-restore mode**: sometimes you want a clean slate without tmux-resurrect replaying the last snapshot (e.g. after a crash with a bad saved state, or on a fresh login where you want only the bootstrap session).
2. **Override mode**: if a 'bootstrap' session already exists (from a previous login), the current behavior silently skips re-bootstrapping. An --override flag should tear down and replace the existing session rather than bailing out.

The machine-specific complexity lives in `hosts/<machine>/.config/tmux/bootstrap.sh` — the flags must work without touching those files, since they are host-specific and not part of the shared script surface.

This bead depends on bs-cz1 (the unified `bs` CLI), because the tmux-start entrypoint and its flags should eventually be reachable as `bs tmux start [--no-restore] [--override]` once that CLI exists. Until bs-cz1 ships, the flags land directly on the `tmux-start` shell script.

## What

Add two optional flags to `dotfiles/.config/tmux/bin/tmux-start`:

- `--no-restore`: skip the tmux-restore step entirely (do not call tmux-restore)
- `--override`: if a tmux session named 'bootstrap' already exists, kill it and re-run tmux-bootstrap

Both flags are independent and composable (`tmux-start --no-restore --override` should work).

## Acceptance Criteria

- `tmux-start` accepts `--no-restore` and skips calling tmux-restore when set
- `tmux-start` accepts `--override` and kills any existing 'bootstrap' session before running tmux-bootstrap
- Both flags can be combined
- `tmux-start --help` (or with no args showing usage) documents both flags
- Existing default behavior (no flags) is unchanged
- Machine-specific `bootstrap.sh` files are not modified

title: Migrate Brewfile tools to mise and create dotfiles/.mise.toml
description:
## Why

The Brewfile currently manages versioned runtimes and CLIs that mise handles better (version pinning, per-project overrides, shim-based activation). Many tools are *already* in mise but still present in the Brewfile as dead weight. This task cleans that up.

## What

Audit result (from bs-4ea):

**Already managed by mise, still in Brewfile — remove from Brewfile:**
- `brew nvm` → mise already manages node (20.20.2 + 24.14.1)
- `brew pyenv` → mise already manages python (3.12.13)
- `brew jq` → mise already manages jq (1.8.1)
- `brew just` → mise already manages just (1.48.1)
- `go cmd/go` + `go cmd/gofmt` → mise already manages go (1.26.2)
- `uv tox, with: ["tox-uv"]` → mise already manages uv (0.10.9); tox should be installed via uv

**Add to mise, then remove from Brewfile:**
- `brew helm` → mise helm plugin
- `brew kubernetes-cli` → mise kubectl plugin
- `brew terraform` → mise terraform (or opentofu) plugin
- `brew terragrunt` → mise terragrunt plugin
- `cask microsoft-openjdk` → mise java (temurin, latest LTS)
- `cask microsoft-openjdk@17` → mise java@17 (temurin)

**Evaluate (stretch goal):**
- `coursier/formulas/coursier` → mise coursier/scala plugin (if backend exists and is stable)

**Keep in Homebrew (no change):**
- All GUI casks except Java ones
- System tools: coreutils, zsh, git, vim, tmux, fzf, zoxide, tree, telnet, nmap, gcc
- Custom-tap tools: beads, prek, gascity, k9s, astro
- Tools where mise backend is absent or unreliable: kubectx, kind, act, dolt, delta, d2, lazygit, gh, gemini-cli, terminal-notifier, flock, mas, mise itself

## Steps

1. **Create `dotfiles/.mise.toml`** — this will be symlinked to `~/.config/mise/config.toml`
   - Port current `~/.config/mise/config.toml` content (go, rust)
   - Add: node (pin to 20 LTS + 24 as alias?), python (3.12), jq, just, uv, golangci-lint, bun
   - Add: helm, kubectl, terraform, terragrunt, java (temurin LTS), java@17
   - Add `min_version_age = "7d"` (or equivalent mise setting) to avoid zero-day installs

2. **Update `scripts/install.sh`** — add symlink rule for `.mise.toml` → `~/.config/mise/config.toml` (check if `~/.config/mise/` dir creation is needed)

3. **Update `dotfiles/.Brewfile`** — remove migrated and already-in-mise entries listed above

4. **Update zsh configs:**
   - `dotfiles/.zsh/nvm.zsh` — gut or delete (mise activates node)
   - `dotfiles/.zsh/pyenv.zsh` — gut or delete (mise activates python)
   - `dotfiles/.zsh/golang.zsh` — remove if mise handles GOPATH/GOROOT (mise sets these); keep only non-mise exports like `GO111MODULE` if still needed
   - `dotfiles/.zsh/mise.zsh` — already has `eval "$(command mise activate zsh)"`; verify it's correct

5. **Test** — run `make install` (or `scripts/install.sh`) on a shell, confirm mise activates all tools, verify `helm version`, `kubectl version`, `terraform version`, `java -version`, `python --version`, `node --version` all resolve via mise shims
acceptance_criteria: (1) dotfiles/.mise.toml exists, is symlinked to ~/.config/mise/config.toml, and includes all migrated tools with pinned versions plus min_version_age; (2) .Brewfile no longer contains nvm, pyenv, jq, just, helm, kubernetes-cli, terraform, terragrunt, microsoft-openjdk casks, or go cmd entries; (3) nvm.zsh and pyenv.zsh are gutted or removed; (4) mise activates all migrated tools correctly in a fresh shell
close_reason: mise config created, Brewfile cleaned, nvm/pyenv zsh files gutted, CLAUDE.md updated

title: Unify dotfile script helpers into a single bs CLI
description:
## Why

The repo currently exposes functionality through a mix of Make targets, standalone shell scripts, and tmux templates — each with their own invocation style and no shared help text. As the number of helpers grows (e.g. the upcoming workspace.sh), discoverability and consistency suffer. A unified CLI entry point named `bs` would make everything easy to find and easy to type.

## What

Create a single executable CLI script at `scripts/bs` (symlinked to `~/.local/bin/bs`) that dispatches to all existing dotfile operations via subcommands. Each subcommand should print concise help when called with `-h`/`--help` or with no args when args are required.

## Subcommands (initial scope)

| Subcommand | Equivalent today | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `bs install` | `make install` | Symlink dotfiles into `~/` |
| `bs bootstrap` | `make bootstrap` | Dotfiles + macOS defaults (fresh machine) |
| `bs macos-defaults` | `make macos-defaults` | Apply macOS system defaults |
| `bs brew install` | `make brew-install` | Install packages from .Brewfile |
| `bs brew sync` | `make brew-sync` | Show drift between live brew state and .Brewfile |
| `bs brew dump` | `make brew-dump` | Write live brew state back to .Brewfile |
| `bs tmux init` | `make init-tmux` | Install/update tmux plugins and reload config |
| `bs tmux workspace` | `~/.config/tmux/templates/workspace.sh` | Open an agent workspace in tmux (see bs-csc) |
| `bs help` / `bs -h` | — | Print top-level help listing all subcommands |

## Design Constraints

- Single bash script; no external runtime dependencies beyond what the machine already has
- Top-level `bs help` prints a formatted summary of all subcommands
- Each subcommand group (e.g. `bs brew`) prints its own subcommand list when invoked bare
- Subcommands delegate to existing scripts — do not inline their logic
- Symlinked via `install.sh` to `~/.local/bin/bs`; script must be executable
- Makefile targets remain as-is (they call the same underlying scripts; `bs` is an additional entry point, not a replacement)

## Acceptance Criteria

- `bs` is present in dotfiles, executable, and symlinked to `~/.local/bin/bs` after `make install`
- `bs help` prints a readable list of all top-level subcommands with one-line descriptions
- `bs brew` with no subcommand prints the brew subcommand list
- `bs tmux` with no subcommand prints the tmux subcommand list
- Each subcommand delegates correctly to its underlying script
- `bs <subcommand> --help` prints usage for that subcommand
notes:
Design spec saved at: docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-24-bs-cli-design.md

Design decisions from brainstorming session (2026-05-24):

- **Language: Go** with Cobra CLI framework. Chosen for: flag parsing, auto-generated --help everywhere, table-driven unit tests, single self-contained binary after build.
- **Name: bs** (short, fast to type). Binary name is a single constant in the Makefile output flag (-o ~/.local/bin/bs); trivially renameable.
- **Directory: cli/** — the Go source tree lives at cli/ (not bs/); go.mod module is github.com/dlstadther/bootstrap/cli.
- **Scope**: This issue supersedes/replaces the original bash-dispatcher approach. Same goal — unified CLI entry point — but implemented as a compiled Go binary rather than a shell script.
- **Build model**: 'make install' will include a 'go build' step (cd cli && go build ...) to compile and place the binary at ~/.local/bin/bs. Go is available via mise (see mise migration bead), not Homebrew.
- **Subcommand structure**: supports nested subcommands (e.g. 'bs brew sync', 'bs tmux add --name abc --cwd ~/code/blah --agent claude') with flag parsing at each level.
- **Testing**: unittest-style tests for CLI components; Go table-driven tests are the target pattern.
- **Version command**: 'bs version' returns the git hash (and optionally a semver tag) of the currently installed dotfiles/CLI, embedded at build time via -ldflags.
- **Machine setup scripts out of scope**: install.sh, macos-defaults.sh, init-tmux.sh stay in bash/make.
- **Repo path resolution**:  env var wins; compiled-in default (baked at build time) as fallback.

title: Implement tmux-notify in dotfiles
description:
Add tmux-notify plugin to the dotfiles setup for automated command completion notifications in tmux.

## Why

tmux-notify can send system/desktop notifications when long-running commands finish, which is especially useful for knowing when to return to a terminal pane. This is particularly valuable for interactive agent workflows where Claude Code or similar tools are waiting on user approval or input.

## What Needs to Be Done

1. **Research**: Investigate how tmux-notify interacts with Claude Code / interactive agent notification workflows — specifically, can it signal when an agent is waiting on user response or approval? Identify any conflicts or complementary integrations with existing notification mechanisms.
2. **Install**: Add tmux-notify (via TPM or manual) to the dotfiles tmux config.
3. **Configure**: Set sensible defaults (notification thresholds, hooks, exclusions for short-lived commands).
4. **Bootstrap**: Ensure tmux-notify is included in installation scripts / Brewfile as appropriate for MBP 2022 and LEMP9 targets.
acceptance_criteria:
- tmux-notify is installed and active in tmux sessions on MBP 2022
- Configuration is committed to dotfiles/ and symlinked correctly
- Research findings on agent-notification interaction are documented (in bead notes or a follow-up issue)
- Notifications fire correctly when a long-running command completes in a non-focused pane
notes:
tmux-notify is installed via TPM to ~/.config/tmux/plugins/tmux-notify (TMUX_PLUGIN_MANAGER_PATH). Plugin was already declared in tmux.conf; added explicit config: 10s poll, verbose on with title, prompt suffixes include > for custom zsh prompt.

Agent-notification interaction research: tmux-notify watches for the pane to return to a shell prompt (detects configured suffix chars). Claude Code interactive approval prompts are NOT shell prompts — they are Claude-specific UI. So tmux-notify will NOT fire mid-run when Claude is waiting for approval. It WILL fire when the agent session fully completes and exits back to the shell. This is complementary to agent workflows: you get notified when the whole session finishes, not on intermediate approval gates.

title: Inventory Brewfile: identify tools to migrate to mise
description:
The Brewfile currently manages a mix of system utilities, GUI apps, and developer runtime/CLI tools. mise is already installed via Homebrew and is the right tool for managing versioned developer runtimes and CLI tools — it supports per-project version pinning, lazy loading, and stable-only releases via a min-age setting.

**Goal**: Audit every brew/cask/go/uv entry and classify each one: keep in Homebrew, migrate to mise, or evaluate further.

**Candidates likely to migrate (runtimes and versioned CLIs):**
- `nvm` → mise manages Node.js directly (replaces nvm)
- `pyenv` → mise manages Python directly (replaces pyenv)
- `go cmd/go` / `go cmd/gofmt` → mise Go plugin
- `microsoft-openjdk` / `microsoft-openjdk@17` → mise Java plugin (e.g. temurin)
- `helm` → mise plugin
- `terraform` / `terragrunt` → mise plugins
- `kubernetes-cli` (kubectl) → mise plugin
- `coursier` → mise Scala/Java tooling
- `uv` → mise can install via aqua/ubi backend

**Candidates to keep in Homebrew:**
- System-level tools (coreutils, zsh, tmux, git, vim)
- GUI casks (docker-desktop, ghostty, slack, zoom, etc.)
- Tools without a mise backend or that require Homebrew's system integration
- Tools like beads, prek, gascity that are tapped from custom formulae

**Acceptance Criteria**: Creation of a follow-up implementation bead that defines a mise config file (`dotfiles/.mise.toml`) including:
1. All migrated tools with pinned versions
2. The `min_version_age` setting (or equivalent) configured to avoid zero-day releases — only install package versions that are a minimum number of days old (e.g. 7–14 days)
3. Removal of migrated packages from `.Brewfile`
4. Any necessary zsh config changes (e.g. removing nvm/pyenv init blocks, ensuring mise shims are active)
acceptance_criteria: A follow-up implementation bead exists that covers: (1) dotfiles/.mise.toml with migrated tools and version pins, (2) mise min-age setting to block zero-day releases, (3) .Brewfile cleanup removing migrated tools, (4) zsh config updates.
close_reason: Audit complete. Implementation tracked in bs-isz.

title: Add tmux-peacock for session color coding
description: Install and configure tmux-peacock to visually distinguish tmux sessions by color. Peacock assigns unique colors per session, reducing context-switching errors across multiple open sessions.

title: Remove vestigial ansible/ and system/ directories from local repo
description: ansible/ and system/ directories exist locally but are entirely untracked by git — the remote repo is already clean. These are local filesystem remnants that need to be deleted manually (rm -rf ansible/ system/). No git operation needed.

title: Add AppCleaner to Brewfile
description: AppCleaner is a free macOS app uninstaller that removes all associated files (preferences, caches, support files) when uninstalling applications — something macOS drag-to-trash does not do. Add 'cask appcleaner' to .Brewfile.

title: Add Obsidian to Brewfile
description: Obsidian is a local-first markdown note-taking app with graph view and plugin ecosystem. Useful for long-form notes, personal knowledge management, and project documentation that complements a structured issue tracker. Add 'cask obsidian' to .Brewfile.

title: Add Raycast to Brewfile
description: Raycast is a Spotlight replacement with launcher, clipboard history, snippets, and extension ecosystem. Most dev Mac users migrate to it from Alfred or Spotlight. Add 'cask raycast' to .Brewfile. Optionally track Raycast preferences export if it supports config export.

title: Add .tmux.conf to dotfiles
description: tmux is installed and used but no .tmux.conf is tracked. Add dotfiles/.tmux.conf with: sensible prefix key, mouse support, vi copy mode, status bar config, pane/window management shortcuts, and true color support. Symlink via install.sh.
close_reason: Brewfile entries added; shell integration deemed unnecessary

title: Add .vimrc to dotfiles
description: vim is installed but no .vimrc is tracked. Add dotfiles/.vimrc with sensible defaults: syntax on, line numbers, 4-space indent, no swap files in working dir, search highlighting, and leader key mapping. Symlink via install.sh.

title: Add zoxide to Brewfile and configure shell integration
description: zoxide is a smarter cd that learns frequently-visited directories. 'z foo' jumps to the most frecent match. Add to .Brewfile and initialize in .zsh/zzzz_after.zsh (must run after PATH is set). Replaces or augments manual directory bookmarks.
close_reason: Brewfile entries added; shell integration deemed unnecessary

title: Add fd to Brewfile and document as find replacement
description: fd is a faster, more ergonomic find replacement. Add to .Brewfile. Optionally alias find=fd in aliases.zsh or document usage in a comment. Respects .gitignore by default.

title: Add delta to Brewfile and set as git diff pager
description: delta is a syntax-highlighting pager for git diffs. Add to .Brewfile. Also requires a .gitconfig (or hosts/mbp2022/.gitconfig) entry setting core.pager and interactive.diffFilter to delta. This task is related to the .gitconfig management task.

title: Add eza to Brewfile and alias ls
description: eza is a modern ls replacement with git status integration, icons, and tree view. Add to .Brewfile and add aliases (ls, ll, la, lt) in aliases.zsh pointing to eza with appropriate flags.

title: Add bat to Brewfile and alias cat
description: bat is a cat replacement with syntax highlighting, line numbers, and git diff markers. Add to .Brewfile and alias cat=bat (or batcat) in aliases.zsh, with --plain fallback for pipes.

title: Add fzf to Brewfile and configure shell integration
description: fzf is a general-purpose fuzzy finder with shell integrations for history search (Ctrl-R), file selection (Ctrl-T), and cd (Alt-C). It is one of the highest-ROI CLI tools for interactive shell use. Add to .Brewfile and wire up shell keybindings in a new .zsh/fzf.zsh module.
close_reason: Brewfile entries added; shell integration deemed unnecessary

title: Expand bootstrap coverage: CLI tools, configs, macOS defaults, and apps
description: Audit and expand the bootstrap repo to cover high-value items currently missing or unmanaged. Covers four categories: missing CLI tools (fzf, bat, eza, delta, fd, zoxide, direnv, mise), dotfiles configs not yet tracked (gitconfig, gitignore_global, karabiner, ssh/config, vimrc, tmux.conf), a macOS system defaults script, and productivity apps (raycast, obsidian, 1password, AppCleaner).

title: Add tmux workspace template to dotfiles
description:
Add workspace.sh session template script to dotfiles.

## File Layout

dotfiles/.config/tmux/templates/workspace.sh

Tracked in dotfiles and symlinked to ~/.config/tmux/templates/workspace.sh via install.sh.

## Interface

```
workspace.sh --cwd <path> [--name <window-name>] [--agent <agent>]
```

- `--cwd`     Required. Working directory for all panes.
- `--name`    Optional. Window name override; session name is derived from basename of --cwd.
- `--agent`   Optional. Default: claude. Allowed: claude, codex, gemini, opencode, pi.

## Session Behaviour

- New session     → create session named from basename(cwd) + window named from basename(cwd) (or --name override)
- Existing session → add new window; auto-increment name if no --name given
- Always focuses the newly created window.

## Layout (modelled on hosts/mbp2022/.config/tmux/bootstrap.sh)

Single window, 3 panes:
- Left pane (~60%): stages agent command (no Enter — leaves user in control)
- Top-right pane:  runs `git pull origin main && ls -al && bd ready`
- Bottom-right pane: runs `lazygit`
- Focus returns to left pane

## Acceptance Criteria

- workspace.sh present in dotfiles and symlinked to ~/.config/tmux/templates/workspace.sh
- Script is executable
- --cwd is required; missing it prints usage and exits 1
- --agent rejects values outside the allowed set
- New directory creates a named tmux session with the 3-pane layout
- Existing session name adds a new window instead of failing
- Left pane has agent command staged (not yet run)

title: Add tmux persistence scripts to dotfiles
description:
Add the three tmux persistence bin scripts and bootstrap example to dotfiles, following the pattern from the tmux field guide.

Reference: file:///Users/dlstadther/Downloads/tmux-guide_1.html (Persistence tab)

## File Layout

dotfiles/.config/tmux/bin/tmux-restore
dotfiles/.config/tmux/bin/tmux-bootstrap
dotfiles/.config/tmux/bin/tmux-start
dotfiles/.config/tmux/bootstrap.example.sh

All bin scripts should be tracked in dotfiles and symlinked into ~/.config/tmux/bin/ via install.sh.
bootstrap.sh is machine-local and gitignored (only bootstrap.example.sh is tracked).
Add ~/.config/tmux/bin to PATH in dotfiles/.zsh/0_path.zsh or equivalent.

## tmux-restore

Path: ~/.config/tmux/bin/tmux-restore
Purpose: trigger tmux-resurrect restore from CLI without needing to be in tmux and hit a key binding.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Trigger tmux-resurrect restore from the command line.
# Must be run from inside a running tmux session.

if ! tmux info &>/dev/null; then
  echo "error: tmux is not running. Start tmux first." >&2
  exit 1
fi

RESURRECT_DIR="${XDG_DATA_HOME:-$HOME/.local/share}/tmux/resurrect"
RESURRECT_SCRIPT="$HOME/.tmux/plugins/tmux-resurrect/scripts/restore.sh"

if [[ ! -f "$RESURRECT_SCRIPT" ]]; then
  RESURRECT_SCRIPT="$HOME/.config/tmux/plugins/tmux-resurrect/scripts/restore.sh"
fi

if [[ ! -f "$RESURRECT_SCRIPT" ]]; then
  echo "error: tmux-resurrect not found. Is the plugin installed?" >&2
  exit 1
fi

echo "Restoring tmux session from last snapshot..."
tmux run-shell "$RESURRECT_SCRIPT"
echo "Done. Use prefix + Ctrl+r inside tmux if this had no effect."

## tmux-bootstrap

Path: ~/.config/tmux/bin/tmux-bootstrap
Purpose: run the machine-local bootstrap.sh to create always-on sessions. Safe to run multiple times (sessions that exist are skipped).

#!/usr/bin/env bash
if ! tmux info &>/dev/null; then
  echo "error: tmux is not running. Start tmux first." >&2
  exit 1
fi

BOOTSTRAP="$HOME/.config/tmux/bootstrap.sh"

if [[ ! -f "$BOOTSTRAP" ]]; then
  echo "error: $BOOTSTRAP not found." >&2
  echo "  Copy bootstrap.example.sh to bootstrap.sh and fill it in." >&2
  exit 1
fi

echo "Running bootstrap..."
bash "$BOOTSTRAP"
echo "Bootstrap complete."

## tmux-start

Path: ~/.config/tmux/bin/tmux-start
Purpose: combo command — restore then bootstrap. Call once after reboot.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
if ! tmux info &>/dev/null; then
  echo "error: tmux is not running." >&2
  echo "  Start tmux first: tmux new-session -d -s main" >&2
  exit 1
fi

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"

echo "==> Step 1: Restoring sessions..."
"$SCRIPT_DIR/tmux-restore"

sleep 2

echo ""
echo "==> Step 2: Running bootstrap..."
"$SCRIPT_DIR/tmux-bootstrap"

echo ""
echo "All done. Attach with: tmux attach"

## bootstrap.example.sh

Path: ~/.config/tmux/bootstrap.example.sh
Purpose: template for machine-local session setup. Tracked in dotfiles; user copies to bootstrap.sh on each machine and fills in.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Machine-local bootstrap — copy to bootstrap.sh and customise.
# bootstrap.sh is gitignored. This example file is tracked instead.
#
# Run via: tmux-bootstrap  or  tmux-start

TEMPLATES="$HOME/.config/tmux/templates"

# Example: monitoring session
if ! tmux has-session -t "monitor" 2>/dev/null; then
  tmux new-session -d -s "monitor" -c "$HOME"
  tmux rename-window -t "monitor:1" "sys"
  tmux send-keys -t "monitor:sys" "btop" Enter
fi

# Example: notes session
if ! tmux has-session -t "notes" 2>/dev/null; then
  tmux new-session -d -s "notes" -c "$HOME/notes"
  tmux rename-window -t "notes:1" "notes"
fi

# Example: project session via workspace template
if ! tmux has-session -t "myproject" 2>/dev/null; then
  bash "$TEMPLATES/workspace.sh" "$HOME/projects/myproject"
fi

## .gitignore addition

Add to dotfiles/.gitignore (or the relevant ignore file):
.config/tmux/bootstrap.sh

## install.sh additions

- Symlink all three bin scripts into ~/.config/tmux/bin/ (create dir if needed, chmod +x)
- Symlink bootstrap.example.sh into ~/.config/tmux/bootstrap.example.sh
- Do NOT symlink bootstrap.sh

## Acceptance Criteria

- All three bin scripts present in dotfiles and symlinked to ~/.config/tmux/bin/
- Scripts are executable (chmod +x)
- bootstrap.example.sh tracked and symlinked
- bootstrap.sh in .gitignore
- ~/.config/tmux/bin on PATH
- tmux-start runs without errors when tmux is running

title: Configure tmux dotfiles
description:
Iterative setup of tmux config in dotfiles, worked through together with user to get preferences right.

Scope: add/update dotfiles/.tmux.conf (or equivalent) with user-preferred settings.

Known items to address:
- Add tmux-specific zsh aliases (e.g. attach, new session shortcuts)

Discovery reference: file:///Users/dlstadther/Downloads/tmux-guide_1.html

Guide sections and disposition:
- Core Concepts: background only, no action needed
- Commands (key bindings): reference during config work
- tmux.conf baseline:
  - KEEP ctrl+b prefix (do NOT remap)
  - ADD: mouse on, history-limit 50000, base-index 1, pane-base-index 1, renumber-windows on, escape-time 0, focus-events on, true color terminal settings
  - ADD: vi copy mode (mode-keys vi, v to select, y to pbcopy)
  - ADD: pipe-friendly splits (| for vertical, - for horizontal, both open at current path)
  - ADD: vim-style pane navigation (hjkl after prefix)
  - ADD: repeatable pane resize (HJKL with -r)
  - ADD: prefix + r to reload config
  - ADD: detach-on-destroy off, bind x kill-pane (skip confirm)
  - SKIP: status-position top (decide during session)
- Plugins (via TPM):
  - ADD: tmux-sensible (quality of life defaults)
  - ADD: tmux-yank (clipboard, no config needed)
  - ADD: tmux-resurrect + tmux-continuum (persistence):
      set -g @resurrect-capture-pane-contents 'on'
      set -g @resurrect-strategy-nvim 'session'
      set -g @continuum-restore 'on'
      set -g @continuum-save-interval '15'
  - ADD: dracula/tmux theme:
      set -g @dracula-plugins "git battery cpu-usage ram-usage time"
      set -g @dracula-show-powerline true
      set -g @dracula-show-flags true
      set -g @dracula-show-left-icon session
      set -g @dracula-border-contrast true
      set -g @dracula-git-show-current-symbol ✓
      set -g @dracula-git-show-diff-symbol !
      set -g @dracula-show-timezone false
      set -g @dracula-time-format "%H:%M"
      set -g @dracula-show-battery-status true
      Note: do NOT set status-left/status-right manually — Dracula owns those
  - ADD: tmux-peacock (per-project window/pane colorization):
      https://github.com/imomaliev/tmux-peacock
      set -g @plugin 'imomaliev/tmux-peacock'
  - ADD: SessionX (fuzzy session picker via TPM):
      set -g @plugin 'omerxx/tmux-sessionx'
      set -g @sessionx-bind 'o'
      set -g @sessionx-window-height '85%'
      set -g @sessionx-window-width '75%'
      set -g @sessionx-preview-location 'right'
      set -g @sessionx-preview-ratio '55%'
      set -g @sessionx-filter-current 'false'
      set -g @sessionx-zoxide-mode 'on'
  - ADD: tmux-notify (notifications when long-running commands complete):
      set -g @plugin 'rickstaa/tmux-notify'
  - SKIP: vim-tmux-navigator (only relevant if using neovim splits)
- Persistence scripts and workspace templates: tracked in separate issues
close_reason: tmux.conf and tmux.zsh added with all specified settings and plugins

title: Audit and update .gitignore for post-ansible repo
description: The repo previously used Ansible but has since migrated away from it. The .gitignore likely contains Ansible-specific entries (e.g. ansible/.venv, *.retry, inventory secrets) that are no longer relevant. Audit the current .gitignore, remove stale Ansible-era entries, and ensure current tooling (mise, uv, install.sh) is properly covered.

title: Audit current machine config drift against repo
description: Check current machine's live settings (Homebrew packages, Zsh config, dotfiles, tool versions) against what's defined in the repo. Identify any drift — packages installed but not in Brewfile, configs in ~ not symlinked from repo, or repo files not deployed. Goal: catch manual changes made outside of the install/Ansible workflow before they're lost.
close_reason: Audited all live config against repo: Brewfile updated from live state, added mise.zsh, fixed 0_path.zsh, brought in .gitconfig, tmux.conf, and ssh config to hosts/mbp2022. Remaining drift is unlinked files — resolved by running install.sh.
